## [00:00 - 03:01]

So this video is about an alternative path. In the last video I talked about the PS path, the problem solution path, and 90 or 95% of PLF owners should use the PS path. But there are some cases that don't fit so well into the PS path, and it's mostly for artists. And I've had a lot of artists that could be recording artists, they could be authors, they could be painters, sculptors, go through PLF, and actually many of them have great success with PLF. And the path is usually a little different from that problem solution path. If you're selling a painting, you probably don't have a problem solution path. But launches do work, they've worked for PLF owners, and if you take a look at Hollywood, what they do for films is pretty amazing when they launch a film. Or for example, when the Harry Potter books came out, they had people lined up at bookstores at midnight waiting for that book to come out. So launching a piece of art absolutely can and does work. So there's three primary ways to think about this, three primary types of product, of pre-launch content. And one thing is you don't necessarily need three pieces of pre-launch content when you're going down this alternate path, although three is a great number. So I wanna talk about three types of pre-launch content, three types, the three things you could use on this alternate path. And the first one is to foreshadow the work. So this can work really well with novels. It could work really well with non-fiction books. It could really work really well with record, like if you were a recording artist with music, you could release the first chapter. You could foreshadow, you could release a piece of the song. You could release the first song on an album. So foreshadowing the work can be very, very powerful. The second one is the story of the work. And I'm gonna give you the third one because they are very intertwined. And often you'll be using, it's rare to use just one of these two. One of them, or the final one is a story of the artist. And if you are an artist and you're at home in your studio, slaving away on a painting and you're just covered with paint, and it's just not all that glamorous looking on the inside. The thing to remember is on the outside, someone looking at your life, at your work,

## [03:01 - 04:35]

it does seem pretty glamorous. So sharing a piece of how you do this work can be really cool. One of my favorite authors is Stephen Pressfield. There's a book called The War of Art. He's got lots of great books, lots of great fiction. He has a blog where they publish, either him or people on his team publish every week. And he's often talking about his upcoming work and his research and how he's putting it together. So that's the story, he's doing the story of the work, but he often also does the story of the artist, of his struggles, of his past struggles, of how he gets his work done, of how he goes on vacation, how he comes back from vacation. So this story of the work is how the work gets done, what goes into the work, the research, how it's actually created. And so when you do this alternate path as an artist, just sharing some of your life and some of how the work gets done can make for a really powerful pre-launch. And usually you're dancing between these three. Depends on the exact nature of your work, the nature of your art. But we've had great launches and they usually revolve around, they intertwine between these three themes. So try them out in your launch. This absolutely works. It's worked for painters, for writers, for people that did pop singles out. It works. So take these three, it's not the pure PS path. It's often not the pure sideways sales ladder, but it absolutely works.
